skill.name: env-and-assets-bootstrap
skill.description: Prepare conservative environment and asset setup plans for README-documented AI repository reproduction work.
skill.summary: Builds conda-first setup guidance and asset manifests before running AI reproduction repositories.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: Static analysis raised weak-crypto, reconnaissance, shell, and subprocess patterns. The weak-crypto and reconnaissance hits are false positives, but the bootstrap scripts intentionally run conda, mamba, pip, and venv commands and write asset manifests, so publication should include a clear execution warning.

medium_findings.title: Environment Bootstrap Executes Package Manager Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for external command execution. The Python bootstrapper calls subprocess.run and constructs conda, mamba, pip, and venv commands for a user-selected repository. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can execute dependency installation behavior from the target repository, including setup.py or pyproject installation hooks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct subprocess execution is present and the commands can install dependencies from a target repository. The implementation uses argument lists and constrained manager choices, so this is an operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Asset Manifest Writes to User-Selected Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for filesystem effects. The asset preparation script creates asset directories and writes a JSON manifest to a path controlled by command-line arguments. This is expected setup behavior, but users should review output paths before running it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly creates directories and writes a manifest file. No destructive file operations or hidden paths were found, which limits severity.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The reported weak-cryptography locations are descriptive metadata, argparse descriptions, or command construction lines. I found no hash algorithm, encryption code, password storage, or cryptographic verification logic at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic APIs or weak hash names. The static matches appear to come from ordinary text and setup wording.

low_findings.title: False Positive System Reconnaissance Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The reported reconnaissance lines describe asset source tracking or environment guidance. They do not collect host inventory, enumerate secrets, inspect network interfaces, or transmit system data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are policy text about setup behavior, not active host inspection. No evidence of data exfiltration or sensitive inventory collection was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive Backtick Execution Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The markdown and Python lines flagged as Ruby or shell backtick execution use markdown code spans or Python f-strings for readable setup notes. They do not execute shell backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown formatting or Python string output. There is no Ruby code and no shell command substitution at those locations.

low_findings.title: Benign Shell Wrapper Path Resolution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for device-file and shell-substitution concerns. The shell wrapper resolves its own directory, selects a Python executable, and execs the Python bootstrapper. It does not read standard device files or perform hidden system probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wrapper uses common POSIX startup patterns and forwards user arguments to a local script. The external execution is real, but the device-file finding itself is not supported by the cited line.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess-Based Environment Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The script can run package-manager and Python environment commands. Users should prefer --dry-run first and only run it against trusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is direct evidence of command execution. The commands are structured as argument lists, which reduces injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Filesystem Manifest Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The asset script creates directories and writes a manifest file. Users should set output paths inside the intended workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly creates directories and writes JSON. I found no deletion, credential access, or network transmission tied to this behavior.

content.value_statement: AI research repositories often have incomplete setup notes and unclear asset paths. This skill turns README evidence into conservative environment commands, asset manifests, and setup risks before a run.
actual_capabilities: Identifies top-level environment files such as environment.yml, requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, and setup.py.
Builds conservative conda-first or virtualenv setup commands from repository evidence.
Reads declared conda environment names from YAML environment files.
Prepares asset manifests for datasets, checkpoints, weights, caches, and output directories.
Extracts checkpoint, dataset, cache, model, and download hints from README and config files.
Provides platform-specific virtualenv activation commands for Windows, macOS, and Linux.
limitations: It does not validate that dependencies are secure or compatible with every platform.
It does not download model checkpoints, datasets, or external assets by itself.
It relies on README files, config files, and conservative inference, so missing documentation remains unresolved.
Its bootstrap script can install dependencies, so users should run dry-run mode before execution.

use_cases.title: Plan a paper reproduction setup
use_cases.description: Create a practical environment and asset plan before running an AI research repository.

use_cases.title: Document missing assets for a benchmark
use_cases.description: List expected checkpoints, datasets, cache folders, and unresolved source hints for a reproduction task.

use_cases.title: Prepare a safer first run
use_cases.description: Review setup commands and dependency assumptions before allowing package installation commands to execute.
use_cases.target_user: Developer using Claude Code or Codex

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic setup plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a README-documented repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use env-and-assets-bootstrap for this repository. Create a conservative setup plan from the README and top-level environment files.

prompt_templates.title: Find asset requirements
prompt_templates.scenario: Repository has datasets or checkpoints mentioned in documentation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use env-and-assets-bootstrap to identify dataset, checkpoint, cache, and output path assumptions. Report missing or uncertain assets.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare dry-run commands
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants commands but does not want installation yet.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use env-and-assets-bootstrap to produce dry-run environment commands and explain which files support each setup step.

prompt_templates.title: Audit setup risk before execution
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before running package installation against a research repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use env-and-assets-bootstrap to compare README instructions, environment files, and asset hints. Flag unresolved risks before any setup command runs.

output_examples.input: Repository contains environment.yml with a declared name and README links to checkpoints.
output: Detected a conda environment file and environment name.
Recommended creating the documented conda environment before running experiments.
Recorded checkpoint source hints and target local paths for review.

output_examples.input: Repository has requirements.txt but no documented datasets.
output: Suggested a virtualenv plus pip installation plan.
Marked dataset and checkpoint sources as unresolved.
Added a note to confirm asset requirements before execution.

output_examples.input: User asks for setup preparation without running commands.
output: Separated documented instructions from inferred fallback steps.

best_practices: Run dry-run mode first and review every package installation command.
Prefer documented repository setup files before inferring missing commands.
Keep generated asset manifests inside the active reproduction workspace.
anti_patterns: Do not run bootstrap commands against an untrusted repository without review.
Do not treat inferred asset paths as canonical sources without primary evidence.
Do not use this skill as a full reproduction orchestrator or final results reporter.

faq.question: Does this skill run experiments?
faq.answer: No. It prepares environment and asset assumptions before a reproduction run.

faq.question: Does it download datasets or checkpoints?
faq.answer: No. It records source hints and target paths, but does not download external assets by itself.

faq.question: Can it create a conda environment?
faq.answer: Yes. The bootstrap script can run conda or mamba commands when the user chooses to execute it.

faq.question: Is it compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I review before running it?
faq.answer: Review the target repository, setup files, generated commands, and output paths before executing bootstrap commands.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill has legitimate scripts that can run package managers and write files. No malicious intent was found.
